question: What are phantom types and why are they used in API contracts?

options.text: Types attached to interface fields that exist only for TypeScript and have no runtime values
Types that are automatically removed during compilation and don't affect type checking
options.explanation: All types are removed during compilation, but phantom types actively participate in type checking — they're needed to pass type information through generics

options.text: Special TypeScript syntax for creating virtual interfaces
options.explanation: Phantom types are a design pattern, not language syntax. They're implemented through regular optional fields

options.text: Types that can only be null or undefined
options.explanation: Phantom types can be anything. Their essence is that the field is declared as optional (?) and never populated, while the type is used only for inference via infer

question: Which pattern is used to make body required only for POST/PUT endpoints?

options.text: Conditional type in rest parameters: ...args: TBody extends never ? [] : [body: TBody]
Function overloading with different parameter counts
options.explanation: Overloads work, but conditional type in rest parameters is more elegant — one generic covers all cases without duplicating signatures

options.text: Two separate methods: fetchWithBody() and fetchWithoutBody()
options.explanation: This breaks API uniformity. A conditional type allows using one function that adapts to the endpoint type

options.text: Runtime check typeof body !== 'undefined'
options.explanation: A runtime check doesn't give a compile error. The goal is to detect problems before running the program

question: Why is DTO -> domain type mapping performed at the boundary, not inside components?

options.text: So that API contract changes only affect one layer, not the entire application code
Because TypeScript doesn't allow transforming types inside components
options.explanation: TypeScript allows mapping anywhere. It's an architectural decision: boundary mapping isolates changes

options.text: To improve performance through single-pass mapping
options.explanation: Performance isn't the main reason. The key benefit is change isolation and a single source of truth for transformations

options.text: To use snake_case throughout the application
options.explanation: On the contrary: mapping converts snake_case (API) to camelCase (domain), so all application code works with a convenient format

question: Which of the following approaches provide runtime safety when working with API responses?

options.text: Type guards (isUser, isString) with instanceof/typeof checks
Type casting via as (data as User)
options.explanation: as is only a compiler hint, it performs no runtime checks. If data doesn't match the type, the error will occur later

options.text: Validation through composable type guards (isObject, isArray)
Declaring return type as Promise<User>
options.explanation: Return type declaration is a compile-time check. At runtime, fetch still returns any, and the declared type won't protect against incorrect data

question: What happens when using satisfies with a query schema?

options.text: TypeScript checks that the object conforms to QuerySchema but preserves literal value types
The object will be cast to QuerySchema type with loss of literal types
options.explanation: This describes the behavior of a type annotation (: QuerySchema). satisfies checks conformance but preserves narrowed literal types

options.text: TypeScript will create a new type based on the object and QuerySchema
options.explanation: satisfies doesn't create new types. It checks compatibility and preserves the inferred object type

options.text: An error will occur because satisfies can't be used with interfaces
options.explanation: satisfies works with any types, including interfaces. It's a compatibility check operator
